response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: At my age, I have learned that not everything we see or read is true, and this applies especially to social media. Working at the café here in Milan, I hear many customers talking about things they saw online, and sometimes I worry because the information sounds strange or too alarming. I only check social media a few times each week, mostly to see photos from my four children and grandchildren, but even in that short time, I notice how much false information circulates. It makes me quite anxious because I want to stay informed without being misled. My approach is quite simple and comes from years of life experience. First, I never share something immediately, even if it seems important. I take time to think about whether it makes sense. If a story seems too shocking or too good to be true, I have learned it probably is not accurate. I often ask my children or younger colleagues at work to help me verify things, especially when it involves health information or news about the Church, which matters deeply to me. I also try to stick to sources I recognize and trust. When I see news, I check if it comes from established newspapers or television stations that have been around for decades. This might seem old-fashioned, but it has served me well. I remember when we only had newspapers and radio, and journalists had to be more careful about what they reported. Another thing I do is simply ignore content that makes me feel too upset or anxious. I have noticed that fake stories often try to make people angry or scared on purpose. As someone who values peace and routine in my life, I choose not to engage with such content. I focus instead on what I can control, like my work, my baking, and spending quality time with family. My education might be basic, but common sense and caution have always been good teachers. I believe that taking things slowly, asking questions, and not rushing to believe everything we see online is the best protection against being fooled by fake content.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 76-year-old woman with lower secondary education who works full-time as a barista in Milan. Her high conscientiousness suggests she would be methodical and careful in her approach. Her anxious psychological feature means fake content would genuinely worry her. Her low openness indicates she would prefer traditional, familiar sources of information rather than new platforms. Her moderate social engagement and limited social media use (few times weekly) suggests she encounters fake content but isn't overwhelmed by it. Her age and life experience would inform a practical, wisdom-based approach. Her Christianity would make her concerned about religious misinformation. Her role as a mother of four means she likely consults family members. The tone should be warm, practical, slightly cautious, and grounded in lived experience rather than technical knowledge.
response_parsed.tone: warm, practical, slightly cautious, grounded in life experience, conversational
